_description: Book-only content. Never published to the website. Captured continuously across the project; refined and edited during Phase 4 (Dec 2026 - Mar 2027) for the April 2027 launch.

_item_types.foreword: Christopher's editorial introduction. Personal voice, origin story.
_item_types.afterword: Closing remarks if the book deserves one.
_item_types.deepdive: Long-form essay extending an entry. 4-8 print pages. AI-drafted, Christopher-edited.
_item_types.alphabet: Entry for the A-Z Alphabet of Thiccc section. Hard letters (X, Y, Q, Z) need new subjects.
_item_types.commentary: Editor's note annotating a specific entry. Personal, behind-the-scenes.
_item_types.photoessay: Curated multi-page photo spread with short captions.
_item_types.cutcontent: Section featuring deliberately-weak entries that didn't make the cut, presented with editorial frame.
_item_types.origin_note: Short note from Christopher about how/why the project started.
_item_types.acknowledgment: Photographer credits, contributors, anyone owed thanks.

_status_tags.idea: Captured but not drafted. Just a seed.
_status_tags.draft: Drafted but not finalized. Christopher hasn't reviewed yet.
_status_tags.ready: Final. Ready to drop into the book layout pipeline.
_status_tags.cut: Captured then later decided not to use.

items.title: Why three c's
items.notes: Christopher to write. ~1500-2000 words. Origin story, why three c's, why this exists. Most important single piece in the book, sets the voice for everything else. Block 30-90 minutes when inspiration strikes; don't overthink it.

items.title: An Alphabet of Thiccc
items.notes: A through Z. Some letters map to existing entries (A=Avocado, B=Banana, C=Concrete Mixer, F=Ford F-450, S=Sofa Chesterfield, W=Watermelon). Hard letters need new subjects: X (xenon-tank?), Y (yam? yacht?), Q (quill? quarry?), Z (Zamboni, yes, Zamboni). I can pitch options as catalog grows.

items.title: Twelve Plates That Almost Didn't Make It
items.notes: Filter all bookReady:false entries; pick 8-12 most amusingly weak. Present with a short editorial frame ('these failed our standards but failed interestingly'). Auto-curated from the catalog.

items.title: A Glossary of Thiccc Terminology
items.notes: The brand's invented vocabulary defined. 'Thicccness': three senses (girth/density/viscosity). 'Editorial apparatus.' 'Plate' (in the dictionary-illustration sense). 'Rear three-quarter.' 'Brand-coherent thiccc.' Useful book-end content AND brand documentation.

items.title: From the Editor's Camera
items.notes: Photo essay of Christopher's own thiccc photos (taken in his daily life). Rights-clean (he owns them) and gives the book a personal touch. Build as he submits photos via images/personal/.

items.title: The URL was waiting for me
items.notes: Christopher's exact framing in chat: he had owned thiccctionary.com for years and didn't know what to do with it until now. Pull-quote-able for the foreword. The naming-as-omen frame is good: 'A name like Thiccctionary only survives the gap between idea and execution if the underlying joke is robust enough to carry across years.' Either Christopher writes this directly or it becomes a foreword sidebar.
items.content: Christopher's words (paraphrased): 'I've owned this URL for a long time and didn't know what to do with it.' That's the foreword opening. Most domain registrations die in the gap between 'I had an idea' and 'I actually shipped something.' This one survived.

items.title: Borrowed credibility requires intact credibility
items.notes: Useful brand framework that came up while discussing eventual sponsored partnerships. The Editorial Plates vs. Sponsored Plates distinction. Worth glossary-fying because the principle scales: every commercial extension of the brand depends on the editorial brand staying clean. Could also live in commentary tracks for an entry where this comes up.
items.content: Borrowed credibility is the thing brand partners pay for. They borrow yours when their product lands inside your editorial frame. The borrow only works if the credibility was real to begin with. The moment money compromises the editorial, by paying for placement that reads as organic, by softening a verdict, by pulling a hard joke, the credibility deflates and there's nothing left to lend.

items.title: Documenting girth, since 2026
items.notes: Site footer tagline that's actually a great one-line brand statement. Could be the spine, the cover-jacket subtitle, or the back-cover description. Already in production use; flagging as official brand language.
items.content: Tagline currently in the website footer. Recommend including verbatim on the book's title page or back cover.

items.title: A Brief Mythology of the Cement Mixer
items.notes: Surfaced in chat as an example of a print-exclusive deep-dive. Concrete Mixer is the existing 4/27 entry. Deep dive could cover: cement-mixer history (Stephen Stepanian's 1916 patent), the rotating drum's surprising mathematics (slow rotation at 2-6 rpm because faster would set the mix), the cultural status of cement mixers as the platonic vehicle of working-class American labor, the 'concrete bench' philosophical aside (urban concrete that nobody sits on but is somehow important to the streetscape). Brand voice: pseudo-academic, dry, with comedic kicker.

items.title: An-225 Mriya, In Memoriam, At Scale
items.notes: Christopher proposed this in chat as a candidate daily entry; I argued the brand register can't carry the weight of the plane being destroyed in 2022 (it was lost to Russian shelling at Hostomel). The plane deserves coverage, but as a deep-dive in the book, not a satirical daily entry. Brand voice for THIS specific piece needs to find a register that can hold loss without breaking, closer to the New York Times Magazine 'In Memoriam' tone than the dictionary-snark default. Specifics worth working in: the plane's six engines (six!), its design as the Buran shuttle carrier, the fact that 'Mriya' means 'dream' in Ukrainian, the brief reconstruction effort announced and then complicated by ongoing war, the way it became a symbol BEFORE its destruction not because of it. Length: 6-8 print pages. Best as the closing essay of the Vehicles & Transport chapter, or as front-of-book in a new 'Engineering Marvels' section.

items.title: Dictionary, Unabridged, the meta-entry that closes the book
items.notes: Christopher spotted a Reddit post (r/pics 'OC Love my new thicc dictionary', https://www.reddit.com/r/pics/comments/x5r4ks/oc_love_my_new_thicc_dictionary/) of a hilariously brick-sized physical dictionary. The concept: a Thiccctionary entry FOR a dictionary. Most self-referential thing the brand can do. Perfect closing entry of the book, last plate before the index, or perfect FIRST entry of a future volume. RIGHTS NOTE: The Reddit photo is OC (original content); the poster retains copyright. Cannot use directly. Three options: (1) DM the OP for permission with credit, (2) Christopher photographs his OWN brick-thick dictionary (Webster's Unabridged, OED Compact, or any used-bookstore tome). Brand-strongest move because the editor's-own-dictionary closes the loop. (3) Free-license substitute on Unsplash. Recommendation: option 2. Headword could be 'Dictionary, Unabridged' or 'Webster's, Second' or simply 'Dictionary', depends on what specific volume Christopher photographs. The entry's etymology can lean academic (the word 'dictionary' from Medieval Latin 'dictionarium', the book of words, combined with Christopher's specific edition's history). Example sentence ends the book: something about how the dictionary you're holding is, by the time you reach this page, also the editor's dictionary, also yours, also in a sense thiccc.
